summary: Gruber corrects the widely held misconception that Tony Fadell championed the embedded Linux OS project during the iPhone's early development. Through multiple sources, he establishes that the 'dueling OS' bake-off between an embedded Linux approach and a stripped-down Mac OS X was brief (2-4 months), with Steve Sakoman and Jon Rubinstein leading the Linux effort, not Fadell. Fadell was a hardware leader who was promoted to oversee iPhone hardware engineering just as the Linux project's backers departed Apple. The myth originated from a 2011 Businessweek profile and was fueled by the real political rivalry between Fadell and Forstall. Gruber contrasts Apple's decisive, months-long bake-off with Facebook/Meta's four-year, 300-person XROS project, suggesting the comparison reveals more about how differently the two companies make decisions.
key_points: The iPhone OS 'bake-off' between embedded Linux and stripped-down Mac OS X lasted only 2-4 months, not the prolonged rivalry often depicted, and Steve Jobs made the decision quickly based on intuition and taste.

Tony Fadell did not champion the embedded Linux project — it was led by Steve Sakoman with backing from Jon Rubinstein, both of whom left Apple in late 2005/early 2006 while Fadell was promoted to lead iPhone hardware.

The myth of Fadell backing the Linux project stems from his real political rivalry with Forstall and a single 2011 Businessweek paragraph that conflated personal competition with technical allegiance.
Facebook/Meta's four-year, 300-person XROS project stands in stark contrast to Apple's brief bake-off, illustrating a broader corporate tendency toward indecision and FOMO-driven overexploration.
The iPod OS was a fragile mess of code from multiple sources without memory protection, making it an implausible foundation for a phone OS — the choice of Mac OS X was clearly correct.
money_quotes: Steve Jobs was famously good at picking the winning horse early. A few sources I contacted mentioned missing Jobs's decisiveness.
His good taste is famous, but his faith in his intuition was extraordinary too.
There is a certain kind of Linux enthusiast who thinks the answer to any technical problem can be found in Linux.
Fadell and Forstall might not have liked each other, but they were the hardware yin and software yang of the iPhone's creation — winners both, but also neither to last long within Apple.
It is interesting to compare the two bake-offs, but only insofar as how differently they were conducted.
A fear of shutting ideas down. That every idea should be explored in depth — why not, with so much money available to spend? FOMO, at the level of giant corporations, with trillion-dollar stakes.
If you disappointed him, you wouldn't get a chance to ever disappoint him again.
The look on Scott's face was incredible. It was like his daddy told him he didn't love him anymore.
key_insight: The myth that Fadell backed the embedded Linux iPhone OS project conflates a real personal rivalry with a brief technical bake-off that Jobs decisively resolved in months — a speed of decision-making that stands in sharp contrast to how large tech companies operate today.
